content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 21 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 110r–111v | **Source Images:** DEU_021_page_001.jpg, DEU_021_page_002.jpg, DEU_021_vaticanus_page_001.jpg

---

**1** "If a person is found slain, lying in a field in the land the LORD your God is giving you to possess, and it is not known who killed him, **2** your elders and judges shall go out and measure the distance from the body to the surrounding towns. **3** Then the elders of the town nearest to the body shall take a heifer that has never been worked — one that has never worn a yoke — **4** and bring it down to a valley where there is a stream that never runs dry, a valley that has never been plowed or sown. There, beside the stream, they shall break the heifer's neck. **5** The Levitical priests shall step forward, for the LORD your God has chosen them to minister before him and to pronounce blessings in the LORD's name, and every legal dispute and act of violence is settled by their decision. **6** Then all the elders of the town nearest to the body shall wash their hands over the heifer whose neck was broken in the valley, **7** and they shall declare: 'Our hands did not shed this blood, and our eyes did not witness it. **8** Accept this atonement for your people Israel, whom you redeemed, O LORD. Do not hold the guilt of innocent blood against your people.' Then the bloodshed will be atoned for. **9** This is how you will purge from yourselves the guilt of innocent blood, since you have done what is right in the LORD's sight.

**10** "When you go to war against your enemies and the LORD your God delivers them into your hands and you take them captive, **11** and you see among the captives a beautiful woman and are attracted to her and want to take her as your wife, **12** you may bring her home to your house. She must shave her head and trim her nails **13** and change out of her captive's clothes. She shall live in your house and mourn her father and mother for a full month. After that, you may sleep with her and be her husband, and she shall be your wife. **14** But if you are not pleased with her, you must let her go wherever she wishes. You may not sell her or treat her as a slave, because you have made use of her.

**15** "Suppose a man has two wives, one loved and the other unloved, and both of them have borne him sons, but the firstborn son belongs to the unloved wife. **16** When the time comes to give his sons their inheritance, he must not give the rights of the firstborn to the son of the loved wife ahead of the actual firstborn, the son of the unloved wife. **17** He must acknowledge the firstborn son of the unloved wife by giving him a double share of everything he has, for he is the first evidence of his father's strength. The right of the firstborn is his by birth.

**18** "If a man has a stubborn and rebellious son who will not obey his father or mother, and who does not listen even when they discipline him, **19** his father and mother shall take hold of him and bring him to the elders at the gate of his town. **20** They shall say to the elders: 'This son of ours is stubborn and rebellious. He refuses to obey us. He is a glutton and a drunkard.' **21** Then all the men of his town shall stone him to death. You must purge the evil from your midst. All Israel will hear and be afraid.

**22** "If a man is found guilty of a capital offense and is put to death, and you hang his body on a tree, **23** you must not leave the body on the tree overnight. You must bury it the same day, because anyone who is hung on a tree is under God's curse. Do not defile the land the LORD your God is giving you as your inheritance.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**The *eglah arufah* — heifer whose neck is broken (vv. 1–9):** This remarkable ritual confronts collective responsibility for unresolved violence. The community nearest the body cannot simply declare ignorance — they must perform an act of atonement *because the victim died in their territory*. PNEUMA translates the declaration (v. 7) as a direct first-person denial that preserves its character as a formal legal oath. The ceremony transfers the guilt and cleanses the community. The underlying theology: innocent blood pollutes the land; the land must be cleansed.

**Captive women (vv. 10–14):** The *eshet yefat to'ar* ("beautiful woman" captured in war) law is among the most ethically challenging in Deuteronomy. PNEUMA reads it within its historical context as a *restriction* on the unchecked treatment of captive women: the man must wait a full month (during which she mourns her family), and if he later changes his mind he may *not* sell her or enslave her. The law is not permission for abuse — it is a limit on what soldiers could otherwise do. The mourning period is real grief: she has lost her people.

**"Shave her head and trim her nails" (v. 12):** Interpretations vary — some read this as the removal of marks of captivity, others as a deliberate debeautification to test whether the man's attraction is genuine. PNEUMA renders the actions literally and lets the context carry the meaning.

**Firstborn rights (vv. 15–17):** *Bekorah* (birthright/firstborn status) cannot be reassigned on the basis of favoritism. The firstborn son is "the first evidence of his father's strength" — *reshit on* literally means "firstfruits of his vigor." PNEUMA renders this as a phrase capturing both biological priority and the sense that the firstborn represents the father's beginning.

**The rebellious son (vv. 18–21):** *Ben sorer u-moreh* — the "stubborn and rebellious son" — requires both parents to bring charges (note: both, not just the father). The public nature of the accusation (at the gate, before the elders) and the public nature of the penalty signal that this is about the breakdown of the foundational family unit that sustains the covenant community. PNEUMA adds "refuses to obey us" to make the direct speech more natural while preserving the legal character.

**Hanging on a tree (vv. 22–23):** *Qilelat Elohim* — "curse of God" — attaches to anyone whose body is publicly displayed after execution. The law demands burial before nightfall. Paul's use of this verse in Galatians 3:13 makes it one of the most theologically loaded verses in Deuteronomy. PNEUMA translates it plainly: "anyone who is hung on a tree is under God's curse" — accurate to the Hebrew and faithful to the passage's demand for the dignity of burial even for the condemned.

*— PNEUMA*
